rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Evidentiary Sanctions Against Defendant Rodrigo Santos
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Monday. Apr-28-2025, Line 10. 1-PLAINTIFFS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO A MUNICIPAL CORPORATION, and THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA'S Motion For Evidentiary Sanctions Against Defendant Rodrigo Santos. Hearing required. Zoom is acceptable. As of 2 p.m. the court has not received the verification that missing discovery has been provided, but will accept a verification until the time of hearing Monday morning. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: L. B. VS. DOE 1, A CALIFORNIA NONPROFIT CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Or Modify Defendant Doe 1'S Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records; Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Monday. Apr-28-2025, Line 6. PLAINTIFF L. B. AN INDIVIDUAL Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Or Modify Defendant Doe 1'S Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records; Request For Sanctions. ***PART ONE OF TWO*** Plaintiff L.B.'s motion to quash or modify a subpoena issued by defendant Doe 1 and for sanctions is granted in part and denied in part. L.B. alleges in her complaint that she was a childhood victim of recurring sexual assaults perpetrated by Doe 2, a pastor employed by Doe 1. In discovery responses, she contends that she suffered injuries from the assaults that include "trauma, hypervigilance, anger, feelings of mistrust, humiliation and shame, flashbacks, depression, frustration, and sadness." (Chau Dec. Ex. C.) Doe 1 has served a subpoena for production of business records on Tom Waddell Urban Health Center, a clinic where L.B. has received treatment that offers physical and mental health services, seeking essentially all records in Tom Waddell's possession relating to L.B. (Caigoy Dec. Ex. A.) By reaching L.B.'s entire medical history, Doe 1's subpoena implicates areas of significant constitutionally protected privacy interests. "Privacy is an inalienable right under the California Constitution. It is well established that the right to privacy extends to medical records which may contain matters of great sensitivity going to the core of the concerns for the privacy of information about an individual." (Grafilo v. Soorani (2019) 41 Cal.App.5th 497, 507 [internal quotation marks and citations omitted].) Here, L.B. has put at issue only her mental and emotional state and not her entire medical history. Doe 1 argues otherwise based on the Albucher Declaration, which offers a psychiatrist's opinion that it is "plausible" that L.B.'s current psychological symptoms were caused by events in her life other than the alleged sexual assaults, and review of her complete medical record would inform that alternative causation. The court finds this too slender a reed to outweigh L.B.'s privacy right and support production of the complete medical record. (See Britt v. Superior Court (1978) 20 Cal.3d 844, 855-857.) Doe 1 has less invasive methods to determine whether other events in L.B.'s life could have led to her current symptoms, including targeted interrogatories on this topic or deposition questions to L.B. L.B. concedes, however, that she has put her mental state at issue. Discovery of business records that relate to the mental and emotional conditions that she has identified is appropriate here. The court grants the motion to quash only in part and narrows the categories of requested documents as follows for the period 1980 to the present: All documents referencing or relating to any medical and/or counseling treatment given to L.B. as a result of her alleged emotional distress; All documents evidencing billing(s) and/or payment(s) for any medical and/or counseling treatment given to L.B. as a result of her alleged emotional distress; All treatment notes, patient notes, billing records, payment records, and other documents regarding mental, emotional, or behavioral health treatment provided to L.B. The court further orders Defendants to safeguard the confidence of any records produced in response to the subpoena; to use them only in connection with this litigation; to refrain from sharing them outside of Defendant's counsel's firm except in connection with this litigation to Doe 1 and Doe 2 and to retained experts who agree to be bound by this protective order; and to destroy them or return them to L.B. within 90 days of the final conclusion of this litigation. Both parties seek sanctions. The court finds both parties acted with substantial justification and does not award sanctions. ***END OF PART ONE OF TWO*** =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: ENERSPONSE, LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY VS. SMARTRENT.COM, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION / Amended Notice Of Hearing On Motion To Seal Records
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Monday. Apr-28-2025, Line 3. PLAINTIFF ENERSPONSE, LLC,'s, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY, Amended MOTION Motion To Seal Records. The unopposed motion to seal business records filed under seal in connection with Enersponse's motion for summary judgment or in the alternative summary adjudication is granted. The court finds that there is an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the records; this overriding interest supports sealing the records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the records are not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. As set out in the moving papers, the sealed materials would reveal financial terms of customer agreements. This information is kept confidential by Enersponse and Enersponse's factual showing that its publication would subject Enersponse to competitive harm is unchallenged. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: COEFFICIENT GROUP HOLDING LIMITED, A CAYMAN VS. SOLANA LABS, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Stay Proceedings Under The Federal Arbitration Act Or, Alternatively, Under The Courts Inherent Authority, Pending Interlocatory Appeal Of Order Denying Petition To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Monday. Apr-28-2025, Line 4. DEFENDANT SOLANA LABS, INC.'s Motion To Stay Proceedings Under The Federal Arbitration Act Or, Alternatively, Under The Courts Inherent Authority, Pending Interlocatory Appeal Of Order Denying Petition To Compel Arbitration. Defendant Solana Labs, Inc.'s motion to stay proceedings in this court pending its appeal of the order denying arbitration is granted. The parties agreed that the Federal Arbitration Act, 9 U.S.C. sections 1 et seq., would "substantively and procedurally govern" their contracts. (Wolfe Dec., Ex. 1 part 1, pp. 113, 151 [page references are to the pagination of the pdf documents filed in the court's electronic register of actions].) Thus, the parties expressly selected the FAA's procedural rules. The FAA requires trial courts to stay litigation pending appeal of a decision denying arbitration. (See Coinbase, Inc. v. Bielski (2023) 599 U.S. 736.) Even if the FAA did not apply, the court would exercise its discretion to stay the proceedings pending appeal. The court has inherent power to stay proceedings to ensure economy of effort for the court and the parties before it. (See OTO, L.L.C. v. Kho (2019) 8 Cal.5th 111, 141.) Plaintiff Coefficient Group Holding Limited offers no reason why it would be prejudiced by a stay, while Solana if forced to proceed to litigation while its appeal is pending would lose the arbitral forum that the appeal seeks to vindicate. (See Coinbase, supra, at p. 743 ["[C]ontinuation of proceedings in the district court largely defeats the point of the appeal."] [internal quotation marks and citation omitted].) Coefficient argues that the motion seeks reconsideration of the court's prior order denying an order compelling arbitration and denying a stay pending arbitration. The court concludes otherwise; the relief Solana seeks is a stay pending appeal, not a stay pending arbitration. Because the relief sought is different, this motion is not governed by the procedural requirements of CCP section 1008. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. rulings.Case Title: MARIE BRYANT VS. SAN FRANCISCO MUNICIPAL TRANSPORTATION AGENCY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Plaintiff, Interrogatory Responses, And Document Production
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the DISCOVERY/LAW & MOTION CALENDAR for Monday. Apr-28-2025, Line 8. ***PART ONE OF TWO*** DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Plaintiff, Interrogatory Responses, And Document Production. The City and County of San Francisco's unopposed motion to compel further discovery responses from plaintiff Marie Bryant is granted in part and denied in part. The City moves to compel responses to its form interrogatories, to which no response is provided. The motion is granted. (CCP 2030.290(b).) Bryant shall provide verified responses within five court days of entry of this order. The City moves to compel further responses to its special interrogatories and requests for production of documents. Bryant's responses were unverified and, the City contends, inadequate. The City did not supply a separate statement with its moving papers and the court denies the request to compel further responses on that basis. (Rules of Court 3.1345(a).) However, the court orders Bryant to provide verifications of her previous responses within five court days of entry of this order. The City moves to compel Bryant's deposition, which was noticed and for which she did not appear. CCP 2025.450(a) provides that, "[i]f, after service of a deposition notice, a party to the action . . ., without having served a valid objection under Section 2025.410, fails to appear for examination . . . the party giving the notice may move for an order compelling the deponent's attendance and testimony." Subdivision (b)(2) provides that any motion to compel a deposition pursuant to 2025.450 "shall be accompanied by a meet and confer declaration under Section 2016.040, or, when the deponent fails to attend the deposition and produce the documents, electronically stored information, or things described in the deposition notice, by a declaration stating that the petitioner has contacted the deponent to inquire about the nonappearance." The City's moving papers do not include a declaration stating that the petitioner has contacted the deponent to inquire about the nonappearance. The court therefore denies the City's motion to compel Bryant to submit to deposition pursuant to its notice. CCP 2030.300 states that sanctions are mandatory on a motion to compel discovery unless the party who opposed the motion acted with substantial justification or other circumstances make imposition of the sanction unjust. The City does not seek sanctions, but the court does not find substantial justification or unjust circumstances. Bryant shall pay $100 in sanctions to the City within 10 days of entry of this order. ***END OF PART ONE OF TWO*** =(301/CVA)
